data.text:
INT: Mark Verstandig. So it's towards the middle of 1944. Now in your time with the Kolciaks has ended. What's-- what happened after you finished with the Kolciaks? 

MV: No. It's not finished on the-- we-- we didn't finish on the Kolciaks, because they took us out to be shot. That is where we finished the last cassette. And then my wife started screaming, they're going to kill us! And we were the last ones. And before us were the other people from the family in [NON-ENGLISH]. And in front were nine people with this unit of the [NON-ENGLISH] unit. 

And the two were behind us. The smaller one and the larger one. I told my wife here, I've got a plan. I have got a purse which they threw away after they emptied it. And I had picked it up because I knew that behind the line we have got 20 US dollars still, over there, a bank note. I took this up. 

I told her, look, I will give him this. He will took away the finger from the trigger, and at that moment you will run a little further, and I will run, and I will push this young fellow into the ditch. Because we were coming-- we were walking close-- very close to the ditch. I will finish off this young man, and in the meantime you will run away and I will jump into the ditch. 

And at a certain stage I took out-- I took out this purse from my pocket, and I told this tall man-- I took him-- I told him, look. Here I've got hundred dollars, and let us go away. He grabbed it. And I-- at that moment when he grabbed it, I pushed the young fellow, the younger one, the-- the shorter one with the handgun, into the ditch with all my strength. And he-- he was just in-- in the ditch. And a few yards away from him, I jumped into the same ditch. 

I didn't want to jump together with him. And I was sure the-- in the meantime, the-- the old fellow, the-- the tall fellow started to shout [NON-ENGLISH]. Stop. I'm-- because I'm going to shoot. I told him shoot at us. I was already in the water. I was jumped into the water. And my wife can't forgive me that I told him shoot. Shoot at us. 

And she jumped. She came back. She was already in the [INAUDIBLE] to-- to jump into the ditch. She just turned back and jumped on this tall fellow, trying to grab his rifle. He couldn't get rid of her. So he-- he was hitting her with the butt of the rifle, and then she was falling. He just put the rifle-- he-- he was-- put the rifle to her-- to her arm and point blank he shot her. 

She fell down. And she-- he was think-- he-- he thought certainly that she's dead. Anyhow, I didn't know anything. I heard a shot, but I was sure that at that time, my-- my wife was-- is already far away in the ditch. I was lying in the ditch for quite a time, maybe a quarter of an hour. Maybe half an hour. Quite a time. 

No, it was-- it was a quarter of an hour, until I heard one, two, three. I heard the shots in the forest. I knew that all the people are dead. 

And eventually my wife and myself, we were the only survivors. I went out. I started to shout for my wife. Nobody answered. I went back to Kolciak's house. And Kolciak, who was in-- in the yard, when he saw me, he thought that he is-- I am a ghost. And he told me, look, your wife came back. She is wounded, and bleeding, and I put her into another hiding place. 

Because he had another hiding place, where he had an officer there. We knew about this hiding place. And it was in-- in the-- in-- in the barn. You have to find out which-- which boards, timber boards, are loose and move them away. And there was underneath the straw in the barn, there was a hiding place. There was a hiding place supported with wood and so on and so on. 

I put her over there. I came back. I came back, and I could hear-- I could hear her-- her crying, whimpering. Were in-- in pain. I went-- I told her that I am back, and I came into-- I came there. And she told me next day-- because she fell asleep later. She fell asleep. But before she fell asleep, I came-- I went out to Kolciak. And he was-- he was on the floor-- he was out-- outside the-- the yard, on-- in the yard. 

And I told him, look, you watch out here. I am going to bring Valas in. I didn't trust him. So he said, no, you stay here. I'm going to-- I am going to bring Valas. I told him, you better do, but I will not be in there. I will not be in here. I will be watching you bringing Valas. 

He could see that I have no confidence in him, that I didn't want to go back into-- into the hiding place. And after a while-- after a while, I could see them both coming. Valas came back. And they brought sheets, and they bandaged the thing. I could see there was a hole-- a hole in-- in-- in the back as big as a fist. 

Because I didn't know it-- I didn't know it, but Valas, when he saw it, he said the bastard used a dumdum bullet. A dumdum bullet is exploding inside the body. It goes in and inside the body it's exploding. That's a dumdum bullet. You-- the bastard used a dumdum bullet. It's-- it's-- this is a-- this is a war crime. To use a dumdum bullet is a war-- is-- is a crime. 

Anyhow, we decided next day that they will bring-- that they will-- that he will send his brother-- Kolciak will send his son to bring a doctor. I wanted to bring him doctor-- doctor-- a friend of mine. I don't remember his name anymore at this-- no. I don't-- his name escape me at this very moment. 

A friend of mine. In the meantime, they brought in another doctor. But that's not important. What I'm trying to tell you is that when my wife came back and Kolciak said that he put her into the other-- into the other hiding place, that was a lie. She came back, and he just bodily threw her out from the house. 

And this was my wife bleeding. She remembered that there is a hiding place. There she found out-- she knocked each-- each board. She found out which board is loose, and she crawled in. She crawled in. He-- he threw her out. But when he saw me, that I am alive, and nothing happened to me, he got bloody scared. And he brought Valas. Next day he sent his son to bring a doctor. 

The doctor came, not-- I wrote a letter to-- I wrote a letter to Dr. Droba, what happened. In the meantime came Valas, and Valas gave me the names of all the people who were a unit. Because Valas just came in when they were there. He couldn't go back. He came in. So-- and he knew them. 

They are from a-- from a-- from a neighboring village. He knew all of them. He gave me a list of all the name. I sent the list to Droba. And I asked for the doctor. The doctor was a friend of mine. 

I was in correspondence with him until-- until last year when he died. And another doctor came, Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. I never knew him. It was a-- a man from Silesia, who was thrown out by the Germans anyhow. And he was a new man, and he came back. At the same time, the son brought a bundle of notes, of bank notes, for my wife from-- from Droba, because they took away our money, so I asked him for money. He has-- he has our dollars, our currency, and so on. Anyhow, he sent us the money. 

And my-- he was-- that-- the doctor was examining my wife, and my wife was keeping in her hand this bundle of bank notes. When she asked him how much we owe-- owe for the visit, he said nothing. I didn't come here for money. But anyhow, after-- after she told him here she must pay for it, he told her, look. When I go seven kilometers out of town, it costs so much and so much. You pay me that. That was a few dollars. 

And so she-- she gave him the money. And the man, the son of Kolciak who came back after he-- after he returned-- after return to Mielec with the doctor, when he came back, he brought in bandages and all kind of-- of disinfectant which were available, quite a lot. I was sure that it was Droba who sent it. 

In the meantime, it happened that this doctor who took the money went to the chemist and bought this and certainly paid much more for bandages than she-- than he received it, and he sent it back. It shows you some-- some people-- some good people, some bad people. Matter of fact, he was awarded the-- post-mortem he was awarded by the [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And that was that-- that-- he said that nothing happened seriously. That the bullet didn't touch the lungs. It went from the corner, from the elbow here, slided under the skin, and-- and exploded, made a the hole-- this hole-- this hole. Whenever I look at this hole, I remember that guy. 

And that was that. I knew-- I knew all the people who-- who-- who did it. We-- next day we had to go away from-- we had to go away from-- from-- from this place. And we had to go away. And Valas found for us a place, again back to the Markowskis. 

And that was the 31st of May, the night of 31st of May. Went back to the Markowskis, their old place. A day later, or two days later, a bombshell. It look-- it happened that Valas and Kolciak were arrested by the gestapo. 

The question was who did it? And everybody in the family of the Markowskis knew who did it. Valas and Mrs. Markowski's sister, they had another sister, Mrs. Duskievici. And my auntie placed over there a lot of merchandise from their shop, with the Duskievici, for-- for after-- to-- to-- to-- to take-- to-- to-- to keep it for after the war. And among them there was that Persian lamb coat of my cousin Leah, was in their place. 

Now, Duskievici was going around to find out-- he was spying on Valas, that Valas may-- that Valas may be hiding the family which gave it-- which-- which deposited their goods with his mother. And then Valas came that night, that critical night, to Kolciak. He recognized his nephew on the road in the dark. 

He was-- that means that it was the nephew who tipped off the [NON-ENGLISH] people to come and liquidate-- not me. He was interested in the family Ostrow, in my-- in-- in my auntie and her-- and her two daughters, to-- to liquidate them so that they could inherit the goods and especially this-- this Persian lamb coat. 

Well, Valas gave me the list of all the names. And the-- and the suspicion of the family was that they came out and decided to let us live because I am a-- I was a friend of Vladislav Jasinszki of Jedrusie. And they believed it, that we went together to university, together to gymnasium, and-- and that-- that he nominated me [NON-ENGLISH], which means an officer, a no-commissioned officer [NON-ENGLISH]. So they-- they-- they decided that they are not going to shoot us. 

But it was on the instance of this Duskievici that they came back. And besides this, they-- he argued certainly that all-- after all they took away some jewelry. They took away my wedding ring. And I had another ring here. And on the-- that they-- they-- that in case we-- we survive, they will not be-- they-- they-- they will-- they will in-- in any case be responsible for-- for-- for-- for such an attack on us. 

Anyhow, they came back. That was-- the answer is that it was Duskievici who wanted Valas and Kolciak be liquidated so that nobody could point the finger that it was him, Duskievici, who brought-- who brought the [NON-ENGLISH] for the killing. 

INT: If I could just ask you to go back to the time the [NON-ENGLISH] came for you. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: And your cousins. What was the state of mind of the other people there? 

MV: Of which people? 

INT: The other people that were with you in hiding when the [NON-ENGLISH] came for them? 

MV: What was the other people afraid? 

INT: Were they very afraid? Or were they optimistic that they were going to the forest, somewhere else? When they came then? 

MV: No, no. They-- they didn't know. Maybe they were optimistic. You see, everybody-- everybody thinks that maybe-- as a matter of fact, before-- the moment I-- I-- the moment I saw that he took away his finger from the trigger, I shouted to them, run for your life! Run for your life! But they didn't. They felt somehow they were-- they felt somehow paralyzed. 

There were nine people in front of them with guns. I had only-- I was far away in the-- in the back because I slowed down. I was far away from the back, and there were-- I-- we-- we had two people in the-- and so when I shoved the other one into the ditch, there was only one. That's our different story. 

So they were such-- they were-- it's-- they-- they-- they were resigned. I think they were resigned. 

That was that. That was-- that way we came back, and that was the 31st of May, 1944, two months before we were liberated. 

INT: Then you were back at the Markowskis. 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: And can you tell us, please, about that period? Tell us about the period at the Markowskis in the second half of 1944. 

MV: What was then? 

INT: Your period with the Markowskis in the second half of 1944. 

MV: The appearance? 

INT: Your period. Your time. 

MV: Ah, the-- the period. The time over there. They were very disturbed because Valas was arrested and Kolciak was arrested. So I was afraid that they may throw us out because-- but they said that Valas will never-- will never tell the gestapo that they are keeping us. And Kolciak didn't know where we went. 

So they were safe, and they-- they-- they tried their best to keep us-- to keep us alive, and to control us. They were very, very nice people. And they were very nice people. We can't complain. 

And that was until-- we were there until the Russians came very close. One day, they came along. Markowski came along and said, look, the Russians are in the village. There are few people, a few tanks. There were a few tanks. There was-- no, one-- one reconnaissance-- reconnaissance. The have one reconnaissance truck with Russians here. 

If we want, we can go out to them. I told him, look. I'm not going out now because they may go back. They're coming and going. I'm not sure about it. And then it-- as it happens that two days later, they-- they packed. They were in the middle of a barbecue. And one of the-- the truck, the reconnaissance truck, came back from-- from the-- the end of the village, and they said that the Germans are preparing a counter-offense, offensive. 

And they moved away very quick. They said to the people, don't worry. In two, three days we will be back. We can't move there today because we haven't got the bridges ready, and the-- the tanks can't go through. But we have got enough artillery to mince them. And the same-- that was midday when they went back. 

We started to think that maybe we should have gone out and go back with them. But it was too late. They went already. They left already. During the night we could see the German army moving, and too, we could hear them. We could hear them moving along the two roads [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] in the direction of-- to Sandomierz. To-- to the Vistula and the San coming together. 

And there-- a few hours there south of this-- south of the-- the battle. And we could hear-- we could hear the battle of tanks and artillery going on day after day. I mean, night-- day and night. On the third day, in the morning-- on the third day, in the morning, we could hear already the-- the rattle-- the rattle of machine guns. That means the Russians were very close. We could hear the-- the-- the battle was already with machine guns. 

And then something happened again. Something happened again. We were-- it was a beautiful day. There was never-- no more-- no more gestapo. No more Polish police. We were sitting on the loft, and suddenly-- suddenly I could hear a wiz-- a shell, an artillery shell coming close, coming close, one shell. 

My wife said, I'm going back to my-- to-- I'm going back to-- to the hiding place. The hiding place, you have to go back from the loft. There was a ladder, step-ladder. There was a ladder down. I'm going back. I said to her, look. You're not going back. And I said I'm going back. 

So she went back. She was halfway. The-- only the head was out. And I heard another whiff, another shell coming. And I felt it is coming like to-- to the south. I jumped and brought my wife out with all my strength from this ladder, back to the loft, because in case of a fire, it will be too difficult to go up from the hiding place on the ladder to the loft and then with the ladder down to the hole. So I pulled her back with all my strength. 

And when she was there, we could hear a-- we could hear the-- a-- a shell-- a shell exploding. The house was shaking. I looked over that way. I looked the direction of the-- of the hiding place, and I could see light. The hiding place was dark. I could feel as I came near there, that there's no more hiding place. The whole corner of the house is exploded. 

I looked down. I said there is no more hiding place. What happened was, that the neighbor-- that the neighbor-- our neighbor, the neighbor of Markowski, was a curious fellow. He wanted to watch the exchange of shells, of fire between the Germans and the Russians. The front was there. 

And the observer-- the German observer from the tower of the church of [NON-ENGLISH] spotted him on the tree. He was spotting-- he was sitting on a tree watching the shells. And he was sure that this must be a Russian directing the fire-- the Russian fire against them. So he pointed a couple of shells in this direction. The first shell fell short, but the second shell came just where the hiding place was. And everything in the hiding place was completely destroyed. 

Now we were without a hiding place anymore. Markowski, at that time, dug a new hiding place for a day or two, with-- with the Russians are already-- there-- you could hear the machine guns. The-- the-- he dug a grave in-- in the barn for the night, and covered it with-- with-- with timber, with boards, and straw and so on. They went with-- there we went for the night. 

And next day in the morning-- next day in the morning we went-- we were in the barn because there was no more anywhere else. There was no more hiding place, but we're in the barn. We-- we were out of the grave in the barn. And I could see the Russian soldiers coming from the field, from behind. From behind, they went out of the barn, the Russians there. We-- the only question they ask, are there Germans around here? Don't they know they went-- anyhow, within moments there were hundreds, thousands of them everywhere. 

I approached an officer. I told him who I am. And I told him if I can come home, Mielec. He said Mielec was liberated 12 hours ago. That another unit came from an another-- from the south and liberated Mielec before they came here. And then the afternoon, he said, you can go. When we look around, and-- and if there aren't Germans around here, you can go home. 

INT: What was the date? 

MV: Huh? 

INT: What was the date? 

MV: The 4th of August. Was the 4th of August in the afternoon. We went home. And we were not used anymore to walk. It was seven kilometers. And the walk was not so easy. Because when-- the walk was along the road. And next to the road there were, let's say, a couple of hundred-- a hundred yards, was the river embankment of the Wisloka. And there was the river. 

On the other side of the river were the Germans. And while we were walking, when-- while we were walking, shells started to come from the other side of Wisloka to this place, closer and closer to the road. And so we went down from the road farther away, but then the shells came over there to this place. So then we went back. We said, look, we-- certainly the other place, the-- the Germans are trying to destroy the road-- the-- the road, and they are trying to find the road. So we can't go back there. 

And so we were walking for quite a time. And some houses were burnt out on the way, and so on. And until my wife couldn't walk anymore. I had to help her. I had to. Because we were not used to walking after two-- two kilometers or so. And then suddenly a small vehicle, like a cart, like a box-- a box-- a cart filled with-- with hay-- with hay. 

And was a one-- a one-horse cart. And a-- a soldier, a [NON-ENGLISH] soldier, was sitting-- an Asian-- was sitting on top over there. And I just-- riding this-- this cart slowly to the-- in our direction to the town. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
